did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a pretrained deep learning model (MobileNetV2) optimized via metaheuristic algorithms (Genetic Algorithm and Bees Algorithm) to classify eight classes of skin diseases (including skin cancer) using a Kaggle dataset, achieving 93% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a deep learning model (MobileNetV2) optimized by metaheuristic algorithms (GA and Bees Algorithm) for skin disease classification. It includes a methodology section, experimental results with specific accuracy metrics (93% for GA, 92% for Bees), and a comparison with prior studies, rather than summarizing existing literature.

title: Metaheuristic-Based Hyperparameter Tuning for Pretrained Deep Learning Model: Application to the Skin Cancer Identification
journal: 2025 1st International Conference on Computational Intelligence Approaches and Applications (ICCIAA)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin disease diagnosis by utilizing metaheuristic algorithms to optimize the hyperparameters of the pretrained MobileNetV2 deep learning model. The researchers applied Genetic Algorithm (GA) and Bees Algorithm (BA) to tune critical hyperparameters such as learning rate and dropout on a publicly available Kaggle dataset comprising eight classes of skin diseases. The primary evaluation approach involved comparing the classification accuracy of the optimized models against each other and recent prior studies. The MobileNetV2+GA model achieved the highest performance with 93% accuracy, while the MobileNetV2+BA model reached 92% accuracy. The results demonstrate that metaheuristic-based hyperparameter tuning significantly improves the efficiency and discrimination capability of deep learning models for identifying various skin conditions, including skin cancer.

Mentioned in the title and abstract as the application domain ('Application to the Skin Cancer Identification', 'identification and discrimination between eight different classes of skin cancer'), but the specific experimental classes studied were infectious skin diseases (bacterial, fungal, parasitic, viral) rather than neoplastic skin cancers like melanoma or BCC.

all_task_types.target: Eight classes of skin diseases (cellulitis, impetigo, athlete's foot, nail fungus, ringworm, cutaneous larva migrans, chickenpox, and shingles)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of skin diseases using a pretrained MobileNetV2 model optimized by Genetic Algorithm (GA) and Bees Algorithm (BA). The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories from a Kaggle dataset. Performance was evaluated using accuracy (93% for GA, 92% for BA), precision, recall, F1-score, sensitivity, and specificity. The Softmax activation function was used for multiple classifications.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Skin Disease Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset collected from Kaggle containing images of skin diseases caused by different pathogens (bacteria, fungi, parasites, and viruses). It was used for the classification of eight specific skin disease classes.

use.training: Used for training the MobileNetV2 model with hyperparameters optimized by Genetic Algorithm (GA) and Bees Algorithm (BA).
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (68 images).
use.testing: Used for testing the final model performance (212 images).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were based on descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

Kaggle Skin Disease Dataset.description: Publicly available dataset containing 1,031 images of eight skin disease classes (bacterial, fungal, parasitic, viral). Split: 751 training, 68 validation, 212 testing.
Kaggle Skin Disease Dataset.notes: MobileNetV2+GA outperformed MobileNetV2+Bees and the baseline MobileNetV2. Class 4 (FU-ringworm) had the lowest accuracy (75.0%) due to visual similarity to Class 2 (FU-athlete-foot). Classes 1, 3, and 6 achieved 100% accuracy. The model showed higher specificity (98.99%) than sensitivity (92.31%).

architecture_details.backbone_features: Inverted residuals, linear bottlenecks, ReLU activation functions
architecture_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported (Softmax activation used for classification)

conclusions: The proposed MobileNetV2 model optimized with Genetic Algorithm (GA) achieved 93% accuracy, outperforming the MobileNetV2 model optimized with Bees Algorithm (92%) and the baseline MobileNetV2 (90%).
Metaheuristic-based hyperparameter tuning significantly improves the efficiency and discrimination capability of deep learning models for identifying various skin conditions, including skin cancer.

The MobileNetV2+GA model demonstrated robust generalization performance, stabilizing at 95% validation accuracy and avoiding significant overfitting as training loss remained lower than validation loss.
The model achieved 100% accuracy for specific classes such as impetigo, nail fungus, and chickenpox, while showing lower accuracy (75.0%) for ringworm due to visual similarity with athlete's foot.
The model exhibited higher specificity (98.99%) than sensitivity (92.31%), indicating it performs better in detecting negative cases than in correctly identifying positive cases.

The primary limitations of the study are the constrained and unbalanced dataset, which may affect generalization capabilities, and the focus on optimizing only two hyperparameters (learning rate and dropout) rather than others like batch size or network architecture.
The study demonstrates the effectiveness of AI in dermatology by accurately identifying multiple skin lesions using a publicly available dataset of over 1000 images.
important_remarks: The study focuses on classifying infectious skin diseases (bacterial, fungal, parasitic, viral) rather than neoplastic skin cancers (e.g., melanoma, BCC), despite the title mentioning 'Skin Cancer Identification'.
The dataset is small (1,031 images) and unbalanced, with class sizes ranging from 100 to 142 images, which limits generalization capabilities and may introduce bias.
The model exhibits high specificity (98.99%) but lower sensitivity (92.31%), indicating it is better at detecting negative cases than correctly identifying positive cases.
Ringworm (Class 4) had the lowest accuracy (75.0%) due to visual similarity with athlete's foot, highlighting challenges in distinguishing lesions with similar erythema patterns and textures.
The hyperparameter optimization was limited to only learning rate and dropout, ignoring other potential parameters such as batch size and network depth.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; comparisons were based on descriptive accuracy metrics.
The dataset was sourced from Kaggle, which may lack the diversity and clinical rigor of specialized dermatological datasets like ISIC or HAM10000.
No data augmentation techniques were employed, relying solely on the limited original dataset for training.
The study is a conference paper with limited scope, acknowledging the need for future work on larger datasets and more complex architectures.
